The door to Dova Salter's study opened before Maren could knock. The hinges were silent, oiled within the last week. Everything in the Arithmetician's domain was maintained with this kind of care—the kind that announced itself as negligence to anyone who knew how to look.

"Come in." The voice was the same. Low, unhurried, carrying the particular weight of someone who had never needed to repeat herself. "You've kept me waiting."

Maren stepped across the threshold.

The room had not changed in fifteen years. The same oak desk, its surface bare except for a single ledger bound in grey leather. The same window, its glass thick with age, letting in light that arrived thin and grey and slanted. Bookshelves lined the walls, their contents impossible to read from the doorway—too far, too dusty, organized by a system that belonged to Dova alone.

What had changed was the woman behind the desk.

Dova Salter sat in a high-backed chair that made her seem smaller than Maren remembered. Her hair had gone fully white now, pulled back from a face that had thinned into angles. The skin at her wrists showed too prominently, the veins standing out like rivers on a map of somewhere flat. She wore a shawl despite the season, dark wool pulled close around her shoulders.

She did not stand when Maren entered.

"Sit," Dova said. Not a command. An observation about what would happen next.

Maren sat in the chair across from the desk. It was positioned in the middle of the room, equidistant from every wall, equidistant from every exit. She noticed this the way she noticed her own breathing—automatically, without comment.

"You look well," Dova said.

"I've been well."

"Keeping the shop. Watching the water. Throwing stones at festivals you don't believe in." Dova's mouth curved, but it did not reach her eyes. It had been a long time since it reached her eyes. "You've built something, Maren. A small life. Very neat. Very clean."

The word landed where she had aimed it.

"It serves its purpose."

"Does it?" Dova opened the ledger. Her hands moved across the pages with a familiarity that spoke of ritual—the same pages consulted every day, perhaps multiple times a day. She did not look at what she was reading. "You've never come to me before. In fifteen years. Not once."

"You never called."

"I'm calling now." Dova found the page she wanted. Her finger rested on an entry near the bottom, though Maren could not see the words from this angle. "Do you know what this is?"

"A ledger."

"Everything is a ledger, Maren. That's what you never understood about us. What you still don't understand." Dova's voice had not changed pitch, had not gained urgency. This was how she always spoke: as if she were reading from a text only she could see. "The sea keeps accounts. The rain keeps accounts. Every life that ends in Verantum finds its way here eventually. We are not the ones who kill. We are the ones who record what killing has cost."

Maren said nothing. The chair beneath her was hard, the cushion worn thin in the center. Someone had sat in this exact spot before her, many someones, each one worn down by the same position.

"Your daughter's name is in here." Dova tapped the page. "Has been for three years. Did you know that?"

The room contracted. Maren felt her hands still against her thighs, felt the moment when her body understood something before her mind could catch up.

"Elara."

"The girl who runs your errands. The girl who thinks you sell fishhooks and twine." Dova's voice remained flat, pedagogical. "She's been flagged. By whom, I'm not at liberty to say. But the notation was clear: potential. Latent. Worth watching."

"Watching for what?"

"That's the question, isn't it." Dova closed the ledger. Her hands withdrew slowly, and Maren noticed what she had missed before—the tremor in Dova's fingers, the way they curled inward at the end of each movement as if reaching for something that wasn't there. "Watching for what. Watching to see what grows. And something has grown, Maren. Something that brought others to my door asking questions I don't appreciate."

The cold had been in the room since she entered, but Maren understood it differently now. It was not the cold of the season pressing through old windows. It was the cold of a body that had stopped generating heat, that existed now on borrowed time and borrowed warmth.

"Who came to your door?"

"A man named Kellan. Former Quill, if the records are accurate. He had questions about founding ledgers. About where they might be found. About who might know the location of a ledger that has been missing for twenty-three years." Dova's eyes met Maren's for the first time since she had entered. They were the same eyes—pale, assessing, impossible to read. But there was something behind them now that Maren had not seen before. A fatigue that went deeper than sleeplessness. "I told him nothing, of course. But he didn't come to me once. He came twice. The second time, he mentioned your daughter's name. Not as a question. As a statement of fact."

Maren's jaw tightened. She felt the words she wanted to say pressing against her teeth—the questions, the denials, the calculations of what she might do to a man named Kellan if given the opportunity. She held them behind her teeth where they belonged.

"What do you want, Dova."

"What I've always wanted. Order. Balance. The accounts kept in proper sequence." Dova shifted in her chair, and Maren saw the effort that movement cost her—the way she caught herself on the armrest, the way her breath came slightly shorter. "Kellan has made inquiries that disrupt that order. He's asked questions about ledgers that should not be asked. He's drawn attention to matters that were settled long ago."

"Settled how?"

"That's not your concern."

"It is if you're asking me to—"

"I'm not asking you to do anything." Dova's voice did not rise. It never rose. But it filled the room in a way that made Maren's sentence die unfinished. "I'm telling you what the situation is. Kellan is a problem. Problems have solutions. The Covenant does not provide solutions; it provides opportunities. What you do with those opportunities is your own accounting."

The silence stretched between them. Outside, a gull called—the sound thin and persistent, scratching at the edges of the room. Maren thought about Wen's meeting, how easy it had been, how she had walked away with information that should have cost more. She thought about the Arithmetician's network, how it was designed to anticipate threats before they materialized. She thought about how a woman who had spent her life reading ledgers might have known exactly where to place that meeting, exactly how much to reveal, exactly when to make it feel earned.

"You set that up," Maren said. "Wen. The meeting."

"I arranged an opportunity." Dova's mouth curved again, and this time it looked almost like a smile. "What you did with it was your own accounting. I didn't tell you to come here. I didn't tell you to ask questions about founding ledgers and missing names. You made those choices. The ledger simply recorded them."

"The ledger always records."

"The ledger always records." Dova nodded, as if confirming something Maren had not quite said. "And now it will record this: the debt is settled. All of it. The work you did before you left. The years you spent avoiding the work. The questions you've asked since you returned." She paused. "Settled. That's the word I chose, Maren. Do you understand what it means?"

Maren understood what it was supposed to mean. Debt paid, account closed, the ledger marked with a final entry that released her from further obligation. She had heard this word before, applied to others, watched them walk away from this room with the weight lifted and the world opened before them.

She had also watched some of them come back.

"What are you not telling me."

"I'm telling you exactly what you need to know. Nothing more." Dova's hands folded on the desk, and Maren saw how thin the skin had become, how the knuckles stood out like small bones beneath paper. "Your daughter's name will be removed from the watch list. The man named Kellan will find his inquiries redirected toward other matters. The shop will continue. The life you built will continue. Everything you wanted, Maren. Everything you came here to negotiate."

"And in return?"

"In return, you do what you were trained to do. Once. Cleanly. Without complication." Dova's voice had not changed, but something in the room had—the weight of the air, the quality of the silence. "The Arithmetician has a problem, and the Arithmetician is calling in an old debt to solve it. That's all. That's everything. The rest is just details."

Maren's hands had gone still against her thighs. She noticed this the way she noticed her own breathing—automatically, without comment. When had they stopped fidgeting? She could not remember.

"Who."

"That's not—"

"Who is the target."

Dova looked at her for a long moment. The fatigue was visible now in everything—the way her shoulders had begun to curve, the way her chin rested too heavily on her hand. She looked like a woman who had been sitting in this chair for longer than chairs were meant to be sat in, reading the same ledger until the words had worn grooves into her vision.

"A man named Varen Colm. He has information about the founding ledgers. He has been unwilling to share that information with the proper parties. He will continue to be unwilling." Dova paused. "Until he isn't."

"Varen Colm." The name sat in Maren's mouth like something sour. "Sera's father."

"Sera Colm, yes. You know her."

"I know that she helped me. Fifteen years ago. When I needed—"

"When you needed to disappear." Dova nodded. "I know. The ledger records everything, Maren. But the ledger also forgets. In the right circumstances. To the right people." Her voice softened, or seemed to—a change so small it might have been imagined. "Sera Colm will not be harmed. Her father is another matter. What happens to him has no bearing on her. That's the condition. That's the only condition."

Maren sat with this. The room pressed in around her—the thin light, the old books, the cold that radiated from a woman who should not have been able to generate warmth anymore. She thought about Elara's name in the ledger. She thought about a man named Kellan who had mentioned that name not as a question but as a fact.

She thought about what it meant to be offered exactly what you wanted by someone who had never offered anything without cost.

"There's something else." Maren heard herself speak before she had decided to. "Something you're not saying."

"There's always something I'm not saying." Dova's smile had become something else—something older, wearier, more honest. "That's the nature of the work. The words that matter most are the ones that stay behind your teeth." She paused. "But I'll give you this, because you asked. Because Wen asked. Because someone inside this organization thought you deserved to know."

Maren waited.

"I'm dying." Dova said it the way she said everything—not as news, but as confirmation of something already known. "The cold you feel. The tremor in my hands. The way sitting in this chair has started to feel like drowning." She looked down at her own fingers, curled in her lap like sleeping creatures. "The physicians give me a year. Perhaps less. The Covenant has already begun preparing for succession. Several parties have expressed interest."

"And your interest?"

"My interest ended when I took this position. That's the rule. That's always been the rule." Dova's eyes lifted, and for a moment Maren saw something behind them—not warmth, not exactly, but something that might have been its distant cousin. "But I have one last thing I want to accomplish before I go. One last entry I want to make in the ledger. And I find myself hoping that you will help me make it."

The room was silent. The light through the old window had shifted, grown thinner, as if the sky outside was already beginning to close.

"What entry."

"The one that balances everything. The one that closes the account your parents opened twenty-three years ago." Dova's voice did not waver. "The one that has been waiting, Maren, since before you were old enough to understand what debts could mean."

Maren's breath caught. She did not show it on her face—fifteen years of practice had trained that reflex out of her—but she felt it, the way she felt everything in this room: the desk between them, the ledger on the desk, the words that had been chosen and placed with the precision of a blade finding its mark.

"My parents' debt was settled."

"Your parents' debt was transferred." Dova's mouth curved, and it was not a smile. It was something older, something that had learned a long time ago how to wear a smile's shape without its meaning. "Your parents made payments, yes. But they never finished paying. And the Covenant does not forgive balances, Maren. It only reassigns them."

"To me."

"To whoever carries their name. To whoever inherits their position." Dova spread her hands, and the gesture took everything she had left. "You've been paying for fifteen years, daughter of Ethan and Mira. Every stone you threw. Every festival you attended. Every day you pretended the ledger didn't exist." She let the silence hold. "It exists. It has always existed. And now someone has come to my door asking questions about it."

The gull outside had gone silent. The room pressed in, the walls closer than they had been, the ceiling lower. Maren felt the weight of what she had been carrying without knowing it—the weight that had settled into her bones during years of quiet, ordinary living, the weight that had felt like guilt and grief and the ordinary accumulation of days. It was not those things. It had never been those things.

It had been debt.

"One year," Dova said. "That's what they give me. One year to finish what I've started. One year to close the accounts that have been open too long." She leaned forward, and the effort was visible in every line of her face. "Help me, Maren. Do this one thing. And then the ledger will show zero. Your parents' balance. Your balance. Your daughter's name." She paused. "Everything. Zero. Clean. The way you wanted it."

The way she had wanted it. The small life. The clean ledger. The shop in the Tide Quarter where she sold fishhooks and twine and pretended the water didn't remember every stone that had ever been thrown into it.

Maren stood. Her body moved the way it always moved—economical, careful, aware of every exit—but something inside her had shifted. A weight redistributing. A debt finally coming due.

"When."

"Tonight. There's a warehouse in the Hollow. Third pier from the river. He'll be there after dark." Dova's voice had begun to fade, or perhaps it had always been this quiet, and Maren was only hearing it now for the first time. "The details are in the ledger. The address. The method. The extraction point. Everything you need."

"And the rest."

"The rest you'll learn when you need to know it. That's how this works." Dova settled back into her chair, and she looked suddenly like what she was—a woman running out of time, spending the last of her strength on a final entry that she hoped would balance everything. "The ledger doesn't show its whole hand at once. That's not how debts work. That's not how we work."

Maren walked to the door. Her hand found the handle—cold brass, smooth from years of palms that had held it before hers—and she paused with her back to the room, to the woman who had made her, to the question she had not quite asked and would not ask now.

"The man at the door. Kellan." Her voice came out flat, controlled, saying nothing she hadn't said a hundred times before. "You said you told him nothing."

"I did."

"But he knew Elara's name. Not as a question."

"No." Dova's voice was barely audible now, worn down to a whisper that somehow filled the room. "Not as a question."

The door closed behind her with the same silent efficiency as it had opened. The hallway was cold, the walls thick with the weight of what had been built above them. Maren walked toward the stairs, toward the light, toward the world outside where the shop waited and Elara waited and the water kept its accounts in grey and silver and cold.

She did not look back.

She did not need to. The ledger had already recorded where she had been. The ledger was already recording where she was going.

All that remained was the entry at the end.

All that remained was the work.
